item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I know that the part about long term mass variation effects doesn't make that much sense, I like that article. The premise is quirky, the suicide story is a sharp left turn into serious, but still well enough executed and the "ORNOT protocol" interview is, to me at least, quite funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Yeah, it's kind of a nickname we gave SCP-3620. Spongy. Short for 'Data Expunged'. I know we're supposed to stay detached, but it's hard when you work with one of the animate ones for years, and frankly, 'SCP-3620' is a bit of a mouthful.

You know I didn't expect to read one of my favorite skips on the entire wiki today, but you never know what's going to happen.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Every day 60 tons of cosmic dust falls onto the Earth, so the mass added by extracting stuff from the SCP wouldn't make a noticable difference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As the amount of material produced and consumed by SCP-3620 do not appear to adhere to the standard laws of conservation of mass, any mass shed naturally or removed during the course of testing must be replaced by an equal mass of food to prevent long-term planetary mass discrepancy.

One of the best sentences I've read on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with you. People are taking the note too literally. I don't think it's meant to actually give us the impression that the world is a simulation, but paint a picture of how the Foundation can break people so easily.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really have to wonder how I missed this the first time. I love the imagery, of the 'broken jpg' looking exactly the same, size and all, regardless of where it's viewed from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea of a chicken basically being a glitched instance of a model due to an overflow error. They should have gone with numeric instead of bigint. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I like the note/interview placement, because I don't think that "our world is a simulation" is the kicker here. To be honest, I didn't take the note as any kind of revelation on the nature of the skip - rather, it's a sign of how hard the researcher's worldview was shattered, and that's much nore notable to me. The comedic aspects are very well-done, too. Really impressive for a first skip, and a definite +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The funny thing is that if he hadn't included the suicide note, there would have been at least one person in the comments complaining that the article didn't do the thinking for them (though not phrased quite like that.) I usually am a bit slow picking up on the subtle twists in SCPs, so I tend to cruise the comments to see what kind of nuances others picked up on, and it never fails that someone either hates the last addendum that spills the beans or hates the lack of an addendum that spills the beans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I support Mlister's opinion. It subverts our expectation and it changed the meaning of this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing the sentiment. That note could be handled differently and felt too ham-fisted and overly-silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the mass-out-of-nowhere SCPs are exactly balanced by the mass-into-nowhere SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still, that could be done in a more respectful and clinical-tone-abiding manner than just "here's the dude's suicide note which we put in for unspecified reasons". The information could have been delivered through a section of text speculating about additional unknown anomalous effects or something along those lines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given that the note directly involves the chicken, a sufficiently paranoid Foundation would probably log it on the off chance that there's some kind of secondary effect fuckery happening. Because you never know, and every scrap of data helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm honestly unable to buy that the Foundation would log their deceased personnel's suicide notes in an SCP document. There doesn't seem to be a good reason for that, besides cheap exposition. It also feels somewhat disrespectful in my book. That suicide note broke my sense of immersion because it stuck out to me as being a conspicuously obvious sign that what I was reading wasn't a formal scientific report, but a piece of fiction written for entertainment purposes.

The suicide thing felt extremely unnecessary and forced, to be honest. Having someone commit suicide in response to finding out that the universe is presumably a simulation is, IMO, possibly the cheapest and most ham-fisted way to convey that they're devastated by the realization that the universe is a simulation. The exact same change in mindset could be conveyed in a much more subtle, plausible, and most importantly effective manner - perhaps a short series of notes from the researcher while they're studying the anomaly.

Especially with how wacky the suicide note sounds ("She's a 404-not-found that shits out eggs", "Control alt delete"), I'm not sure if the guy's suicide is supposed to be a serious event or suicide-as-comedy, and the whole thing ends up feeling clumsy and devoid of emotional impact for me.

In addition, I believe all Foundation researchers, even brand new ones, are trained to be able to cope with shock, squick, existential horror, and all sorts of terrible things, since that's what their job has them dealing with on a daily basis. Due to that, I find the guy's suicide rather exaggerated and hard to believe. In the event a researcher's psychological state is so unstable that the slightest provocation might push them into considering suicide, I believe they'd be on leave getting professional help rather than being at work. Forcing someone who is already in severe distress to carry out a lot of demanding tasks isn't ethical in my book.

Not to say that this piece wasn't incredibly humorous and witty ("any mass shed naturally or removed during the course of testing must be replaced by an equal mass of food to prevent long-term planetary mass discrepancy", etc), but those aforementioned issues stood out to me enough to turn my upvote upside down.

Sorry about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am really curious to see the memes you make from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fun article. I'm in the process of making a meme or two out of this, so I thought I'd share a transparent template for anyone else who is so inclined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. I basically just started out with a series of straight lines and bezier-curved 'em randomly in a way that looked languagey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the direction taken here, but the execution ultimately felt muted for me. The idea that even safe-class anomalies can elicit this reaction from someone is a bit of an obvious conclusion, I feel. After all, the Foundation is concerned with containing things which actively defy our established rules and beliefs concerning the functioning of reality, so it's understandable that even the 'least impressive' anomaly forces one to question one's own reality. This is a process that can lead to a tragic end, and it's certainly interesting that this angle has been relatively unexplored on the wiki.

I feel however, that this isn't taken as far as it could have been. It can easily be assumed that this doctor is not the first of his kind, so perhaps seeing how the Foundation as an institution deals with these sorts of cases would quite interesting. These kinds of implications aren't explored here, so we end up instead with the conclusion that, yes, indeed, any old skip can have this effect on people. This ultimately didn't make me feel much, as a consequence. But hey, maybe I'm just as desensitized as the lead researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Minor note: I really like the pseudo-font on the SCP. Is it intended to spell out anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that's a valid point (and Zyn suggested deleting it entirely in her first critique). My thought was that some people will get the subtlety and others need to be smacked in the head with an anomalous chicken before they figure out what's going on, so I decided to keep it. Also, I'm not sure I'm a good enough writer to convey it properly through subtlety.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I for one really like the order of the interviews/suicide note here, and I think that all portions are completely necessary. I love that it's a normal safe-class until the suicide note, and then the address of this by the resulting interview to bring it back home… it's very logical and really packs a punch. The people are realistic, too, and the whole addressing of how normal people react when faced with potentially life-changing 'safe' anomalies? Brilliant, I love it.

It made me think about something in a different way. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but I meant that there is probably a more subtle way to convey that he committed suicide due to the effect of the SCP on his worldview than the explicit note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much appreciate it. Like five people have downvoted it, so obviously my stupid monkey brain is paying more attention to them than the 50+ people who've upvoted it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep. The evidence of SCP-3620 alone suggests that the universe is all a big simulation… but if you look at all of the other skips, that conclusion is contradicted/reached in a different way/easily explained away/etc. So "We're just computer programs" is one way to look at it, in the SCP universe (and that's the idea I had in my head when writing it), but it's just as likely to be "A wizard did it" or "One of the art GoIs did this as a statement about the poultry industry" or "someone figured out how to make software chickens from their Apple IIe into real chickens, kinda" or a variety of other possibilities.

So yeah, don't take the newbie's note at face value.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mental note: Make all of my future SCPs about chickens so I can at least get a +1 from Zyn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing this, in case you are having trouble deciding something about the article, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Enthusiastically upvoted. Put me in the column in favour of the current order of addenda - making this a human-focussed story is a better way of upending reader expectations, and it's done without labouring the point.

Some of the little details are very nicely realised, as well, from the turns of phrase to the focus on conservation of mass. That is a great way to build immersion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I LOVE this.

"As the amount of material produced and consumed by SCP-3620 do not appear to adhere to the standard laws of conservation of mass, any mass shed naturally or removed during the course of testing must be replaced by an equal mass of food to prevent long-term planetary mass discrepancy."

I upvoted for that alone. Too many times I've wondered about the mass coming out of nowhere from skips burying the earth. And the final interview, emphasizing how even "Safe" skips can be mind-shattering, was brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No you didn’t; I changed the names out from under you because I realized there were too many F people in the article. Sorry!

(I appreciated the Dr. Fuckface)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Right, I mixed up the doctor that killed himself with the interviewer. I'd like to think that this supports my argument more than it detracts from it, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought we weren't supposed to take the note at face value because it was written by a newbie with no evidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interviewer is Agent R, not Dr. F

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the article, but I also think the suicide note is unnecessary. It's too on-the-nose spelling everything out - you could probably get the idea that this might represent some sort of cosmic glitch without the note telling you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's all fun and games until someone fails a SAN check.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved this SCP! Read over it in the draft phase, amazing work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The suicide note I personally felt unnecessary, but it's not bad and people seem to like it.

It's a fun anomaly, and the idea that the Foundation has a Ripley's-esque front to snatch up minor anomalies like this is a stroke of brilliance. As are the "$10 chicken" line or the Buffalo Wings XK scenario.

Overall, good work, and +1. Looking forward to more skips from you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Going to have to disagree. Framing the twist as "The world's a simulation!1!!!1" would have been melodramatic and eye-roll-inducing for me. Gives a sense of forced gravitas over something that really can't be proven. Framing the twist as "Sometimes Foundation researchers find themselves out of touch with civilian perspectives, with disastrous results" gives the story a much more grounded feel. It's frankly refreshing that the potential world-altering implications aren't taken at face value, but that the weight is instead given to how people react to said implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well there are too many grand implications for all of them to be real, so having something that suggests the world is a simulation could be real… you know, we could all be in a simulation. But on the other hand, that's just as likely as all of everything else. Too many problems to worry about them all.

Which is why I upvote, and why I like the interview coming after the suicide note. The punch of the article isn't the world being a simulation, it's the world being too fucked up to worry anymore.

unfortunately somewhat similar to the real world ;-;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is terrific. Great storyline and concept and then you throw in the human part of the equation. I could go on but I'll leave it at that. One more thing actually, the thunderclap mixed with an orchestra going through a wheat thresher line was my upvote moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good to see this posted to the mainlist! There's a lot to enjoy in this article, from the quirky anomaly to the dialogue in the interviews. Also I really like chickens. They're pretty neat.

+1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually had the interview and the note swapped in my first sandbox version and Zyn gave the exact opposite of this feedback. After some consideration, I decided that I preferred her version to yours and mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Wanted Buffalo wings so much the extra mass threw off Earth's orbit"

I mean that's grounds for upvote alone.

I like this one, mainly because of the fact that "Oh god the world is a simulation" is enough to get a rookie researcher to commit suicide with the rest of the team going "Eh, even if it is we don't care."

I feel like it does give a glimpse into how … routine stuff like this is for researchers.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it would be made stronger by placing the suicide note after the interview. The real wham of the article is the implications of the chicken, and so it'll help if you build the mystery of Dr. F's suicide first. The note kind of spells it out.

+ Spoilers
- close this

There are a few other world-as-simulation SCPs out there, like SCP-2244 or SCP-1924. However, I still like the basics behind this, and how it's written in general, so I feel there's definitely space for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Three main addressable problems that don't come down to me banging on an anti-trope drum:
– hide block
  • The interviewer should be listed by name instead of as an interviewer, like so:

Instead of this:

Interviewer: So tell us about how you came to acquire this… animal.

Use this:

Dr. Fuckface:1 So tell us about how you came to acquire this… animal.

  • You blackbox the date of recovery here:

"despite having been in foundation custody since 19██-07-05"

But say it's from the eighties here:

"it's been in custody since the 80s"

  • The redaction of swears is particularly immersion-breaking - it just feels unnecessary, and the swears aren't something that'd really affect anyone reading this, in or out of universe.

EDIT: I also cleared your tags because you didn't apply all the relevant ones. I'm not familiar enough with the tags to fix them myself, so I'd suggest waiting for the tagging team to get to this.

Footnotes
1. Yes, I actually counted out the number of blackboxed letters in their name

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP. Thanks to Zyn, DrMagnus, and DrFred for feedback.

Included image is mainly my own work (base photo; most of the SCP-3620 image), with the exception of the gray sphere which was courtesy of Wikipedia user Darkdadaah and also shared under a Creative Commons license: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sphere_-_monochrome_simple.svg
